3: he apostasia prōton kai apokaluphthē ho anthropos tes anomias ho huios tes apōleias (hē apostasia prōton kai apokaluphthē ho anthrōpos tēs anomias ho huios tēs apōleias): 'the rebellion (apostasia) comes first, and the man of lawlessness, the son of destruction, is revealed.' Apostasia — in LXX political rebellion and religious apostasy are both covered by this word; whether Paul means a final cosmic falling-away from faith or a political rebellion is debated. Ho huios tes apōleias (son of destruction) is the same phrase used of Judas in John 17:12 — linking the ultimate opponent to the prototypical betrayer. Apokaluphthē (is revealed): the passive suggests God controls even the timing of the opponent's disclosure.
6: to katechon / ho katechon: 'what is restraining / he who restrains' — the most debated phrase in 2 Thessalonians. The neuter (to katechon, 2 Thessalonians 2:6) and masculine (ho katechon, 2 Thessalonians 2:7) alternate, suggesting either a power/principle and a personal restrainer, or a rhetorical variation for the same referent. Major interpretive proposals: (1) the Roman empire/emperor (the power that prevents chaos — Tertullian, Chrysostom); (2) Paul's own missionary preaching that must be completed first; (3) the Holy Spirit who restrains evil until the end; (4) a divine decree or angel. The deliberate vagueness may be security-conscious rhetoric — naming the restrainer directly in a letter could be politically dangerous.
1: Erōtōmen de hymas adelphoi hyper tēs parousias tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou kai hēmōn episynagōgēs ep' auton: 'Now concerning the coming (parousias) of our Lord Jesus Christ and our being gathered together (episynagōgēs) to him.' Parousia (royal presence, coming of a dignitary) and episynagōgē (the gathering together) — the eschatological assembly of the redeemed at Christ's coming. Episynagōgē appears in Hebrews 10:25 (not neglecting to meet together) and echoes the OT gathering of Israel (Psalms 106:47; Isaiah 27:12-13; Matthew 24:31: he will send his angels and they will gather his elect).
2: eis to mē tacheōs saleuthenai hymas apo tou noos mēde throeisthai mēte dia pneumatos mēte dia logou mēte di' epistolēs hōs di' hēmōn hōs hoti enestēken hē hēmera tou kyriou: 'not to be quickly shaken (saleuthenai) in mind or alarmed, either by a spirit or a spoken word, or a letter seeming to be from us, to the effect that the day of the Lord has come.' Saleuthenai (to be shaken, as by an earthquake) and throeisthai (to be alarmed, troubled with a continuous noise) are the two verbs of disruption. Three sources of false eschatological information are listed: a prophetic utterance (pneumatos), a spoken word (logou), or a letter supposedly from Paul (epistolēs hōs di' hēmōn). The warning against a forged letter explains the autograph in 2 Thessalonians 3:17.
4: ho antikeimenos kai hyperairōmenos epi panta legomenon theon ē sebasma hōste auton eis ton naon tou theou kathisai apodeiknynta heauton hoti estin theos: 'who opposes and exalts himself against every so-called god or object of worship, so that he takes his seat in the temple of God, proclaiming himself to be God.' The self-exaltation (hyperairōmenos — to raise oneself above, used of proud self-elevation) echoes Daniel 11:36 (the king shall exalt himself and magnify himself above every god) and Ezekiel 28:2 (your heart is proud and you have said I am a god). The temple-sitting claim to divinity has historical resonances: Antiochus IV Epiphanes desecrated the Jerusalem temple (1 Macc 2 Thessalonians 1:54); Caligula attempted to install his statue there (Philo, Legatio; Josephus, Ant. 18.261).
5: Ou mnēmoneuete hoti eti ōn pros hymas tauta elegon hymin: 'Do you not remember that when I was still with you I told you these things?' The reference to prior oral teaching (not preserved in any letter) explains the compression and allusiveness of the eschatological schema Paul now references. The Thessalonians had been told this; Paul only reminds them of a framework already established. This is why 2 Thessalonians is difficult to interpret without the missing oral background.
7: to gar mystērion ēdē energeitai tēs anomias monon ho katechōn arti heōs ek mesou genētai: 'For the mystery of lawlessness (mystērion tēs anomias) is already at work; only he who now restrains (ho katechōn) it will do so until he is out of the way.' Mystērion tēs anomias (the mystery of lawlessness) — the hidden power of lawlessness already operative, whose full disclosure awaits the removal of the restrainer. Ho katechōn (the one restraining, the restrainer — masculine participle) and to katechon (2 Thessalonians 2:6, neuter — the restraining thing) — the shift from neuter to masculine is unexplained and has generated centuries of debate: candidates include the Roman empire and emperor, the preaching of the gospel, the Holy Spirit, the archangel Michael, or Paul's own apostolic ministry. The plain sense is that something/someone actively restrains the full revelation of the man of lawlessness.
8: kai tote apokalyphthēsetai ho anomos hon ho kyrios [Iēsous] anelei tō pneumati tou stomatos autou kai katargēsei tē epiphaneia tēs parousias autou: 'And then the lawless one (ho anomos) will be revealed, whom the Lord Jesus will kill (anelei) with the breath of his mouth (pneumati tou stomatos autou) and bring to nothing by the appearing of his coming (epiphaneia tēs parousias autou).' Pneumati tou stomatos (breath/Spirit of his mouth) echoes Isaiah 11:4 (he shall strike the earth with the rod of his mouth and with the breath of his lips he shall kill the wicked). Epiphaneia tēs parousias (the appearing of his coming) — combining two Greek terms for royal arrival: epiphaneia (the brilliant manifestation) and parousia (the royal presence).
9: hou estin hē parousia kat' energeian tou Satana en pasē dynamei kai sēmeiois kai terasin pseudous: 'The coming (parousia) of the lawless one is by the activity (energeian) of Satan with all power and false signs and wonders (sēmeiois kai terasin pseudous).' The man of lawlessness has his own parousia — a deliberate parody of Christ's parousia. The false signs and wonders (sēmeia kai terata pseudous) parody the genuine signs and wonders of the authentic apostolic mission (2 Corinthians 12:12; Hebrews 2:4). The Satanic energy (energeian tou Satana) stands against the divine energeia of 2 Thessalonians 2:11.
10: kai en pasē apatē adikias tois apollymenois anth' hōn tēn agapēn tēs alētheias ouk edexanto eis to sōthēnai autous: 'and with all wicked deception for those who are perishing, because they refused to love the truth (agapēn tēs alētheias) and so be saved.' Agatē adikias (deception of unrighteousness) is the instrument of judgment on those perishing. The crucial phrase: tēn agapēn tēs alētheias ouk edexanto — they did not receive the love of the truth. The perdition is not arbitrary but consequential: they refused what was offered.
11: kai dia touto pempei autois ho theos energeian planēs eis to pisteusai autous tō pseudei: 'Therefore God sends them a strong delusion (energeian planēs), so that they may believe what is false.' Energeia planēs (energetic delusion, the working of error) — God's judicial act of confirming the unbelievers in their choice. The OT precedent: God hardened Pharaoh's heart (Exodus 4:21; 2 Thessalonians 7:3; 2 Thessalonians 10:1); Isaiah spoke God's hardening of Israel (Isaiah 6:9-10). The delusion is not arbitrary but the judicial ratification of the prior rejection of truth (2 Thessalonians 2:10).
12: hina krithōsin pantes hoi mē pisteusantes tē alētheia alla eudokēsantes tē adikia: 'in order that all may be condemned who did not believe the truth but had pleasure in (eudokēsantes) unrighteousness.' The two-part description of the condemned: they did not believe the truth (a cognitive/volitional failure) and they took pleasure in unrighteousness (a moral/affective orientation). The eudokia (pleasure, delight) they chose was in wickedness rather than in God's own eudokia (good pleasure, 2 Thessalonians 2:11 of 2 Thessalonians 1: eudokian agathōsynēs).
13: Hēmeis de opheilomen eucharistein tō theō pantote peri hymōn adelphoi ēgapēmenoi hypo kyriou hoti heilato hymas ho theos ap' archēs eis sōtērian en hagiasmō pneumatos kai pistei alētheias: 'But we ought always to give thanks to God for you, brothers beloved by the Lord, because God chose you as the firstfruits (ap' archēs — from the beginning; some MSS read aparchēn — firstfruits) to be saved, through sanctification by the Spirit and belief in truth.' The election formula: heilato hymas ho theos (God chose you). En hagiasmō pneumatos (in sanctification of the Spirit) — the Spirit's work of setting apart, which is the mechanism of election's application. Pistei alētheias (faith/belief in truth) stands against the pseudei (the false/the lie) of 2 Thessalonians 2:11.
14: eis ho ekalesen hymas dia tou euangeliou hēmōn eis peripoiēsin doxēs tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou: 'To this he called you through our gospel, so that you may obtain the glory (peripoiēsin doxēs) of our Lord Jesus Christ.' Peripoiēsis (obtaining, securing, acquisition) — the prize secured for the elect. Doxēs tou kyriou Iēsou Christou: the glory of Christ (the Shekinah-glory of the exalted Lord) is what the called receive. This echoes Romans 8:30: those he called he also justified, and those he justified he also glorified.
15: Ara oun adelphoi stēkete kai krateite tas paradoseis has edidachthēte eite dia logou eite di' epistolēs hēmōn: 'So then, brothers, stand firm (stēkete) and hold to the traditions (paradoseis) that you were taught by us, either by our spoken word or by our letter.' Paradoseis (traditions — from paradidōmi: to hand over, to transmit) is the technical term for transmitted authoritative teaching. In rabbinic Judaism, the oral Torah was transmitted as qabbalah (that which is received); Paul uses the same transmission-reception language (1 Corinthians 15:3: I delivered to you as of first importance what I also received). The double source — oral word and letter — establishes the complementary authority of both.
16: Autos de ho kyrios hēmōn Iēsous Christos kai [ho] theos ho patēr hēmōn ho agapēsas hēmas kai dous paraklēsin aiōnian kai elpida agathēn en chariti: 'Now may our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God our Father, who loved us and gave us eternal comfort (paraklēsin aiōnian) and good hope through grace.' The benediction inverts the normal order (Father then Son) to Christ then Father — an unusual christological emphasis. Paraklēsis aiōnia (eternal encouragement/comfort) contrasts with the temporary comfort of earthly sources; the eschatological good hope (elpis agathē) is the hope of the glory described in 2 Thessalonians 2:14.
17: parakalesai hymōn tas kardias kai stērixai en panti ergō kai logō agathō: 'comfort your hearts and establish them in every good work and word.' Stērixai (to establish, to make firm, to set solid) — the same verb Paul used for his desire to visit the Thessalonians (1 Thessalonians 3:2: to establish and exhort you in your faith). The divine stabilizing work is the eschatological anchor against the shaking (saleuthenai) of 2 Thessalonians 2:2.
